content:
# Numbers Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 153-154 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0115-0116
**Source Images:** `NUM_006_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_006_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses:

**2** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and say to them: When a man or woman makes a special vow -- the vow of a Nazirite -- to set themselves apart for YHWH,

**3** they must abstain from wine and any other fermented drink. They must not drink vinegar made from wine or from any other fermented drink. They must not drink any juice pressed from grapes, and they must not eat grapes, whether fresh or dried.

**4** For the entire duration of their dedication, they must not eat anything that comes from the grapevine -- from the unripe seeds to the outer skin.

**5** For the entire duration of their vow of separation, no razor is to touch their head. Until the period of their dedication to YHWH is complete, they are holy; they must let the hair of their head grow long and free.

**6** Throughout the entire period of their dedication to YHWH, they must not go near a dead body.

**7** They must not make themselves ritually unclean even for their father or mother, brother or sister, when they die, because the sign of their dedication to God is upon their head.

**8** Throughout the entire period of their dedication, they are holy to YHWH.

**9** If someone suddenly dies in their presence, defiling the head that bears their dedication, they must shave their head on the day of their purification -- on the seventh day they must shave it.

**10** Then on the eighth day they must bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

**11** The priest is to offer one as a purification offering and the other as a burnt offering, and make atonement for them because they incurred guilt through contact with the dead. That same day they must reconsecrate their head.

**12** They must rededicate themselves to YHWH for the full period of their separation and bring a male lamb in its first year as a guilt offering. The previous days do not count, because their dedication was defiled.

**13** Now this is the procedure for the Nazirite when the period of their dedication is complete. They are to be brought to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting,

**14** and they are to present their offering to YHWH: one male lamb in its first year without defect as a burnt offering, one female lamb in its first year without defect as a purification offering, and one ram without defect as a peace offering,

**15** along with a basket of unleavened bread -- loaves of fine flour mixed with oil and unleavened wafers spread with oil -- together with their accompanying grain offerings and drink offerings.

**16** The priest is to present these before YHWH and offer the purification offering and the burnt offering.

**17** He is to offer the ram as a peace offering to YHWH, along with the basket of unleavened bread. The priest is also to offer the accompanying grain offering and drink offering.

**18** Then the Nazirite must shave their dedicated head at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, take the hair from their dedicated head, and place it on the fire that burns under the peace offering.

**19** After the Nazirite has shaved off their dedicated hair, the priest is to take the boiled shoulder of the ram, one unleavened loaf from the basket, and one unleavened wafer, and place them in the hands of the Nazirite.

**20** Then the priest is to elevate them as a wave offering before YHWH. This is a holy portion belonging to the priest, in addition to the breast of the wave offering and the thigh of the contribution. After that, the Nazirite may drink wine.

**21** This is the required procedure for the Nazirite who makes a vow. Their offering to YHWH for their dedication is to follow this procedure, in addition to whatever else they can afford. They must carry out the full requirements of the vow they have made, according to the procedure for their dedication."

**22** YHWH spoke to Moses:

**23** "Speak to Aaron and his sons and say to them: This is how you are to bless the Sons of Israel. Say to them:

**24** 'May YHWH bless you and protect you.

**25** May YHWH make his face shine upon you and show you grace.

**26** May YHWH lift up his face toward you and give you peace.'

**27** In this way they will place my name upon the Sons of Israel, and I myself will bless them."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 2 -- "the vow of a Nazirite"** (Heb. *neder nazir*): The word *nazir* derives from the root *nzr*, meaning "to separate" or "to consecrate." The Nazirite is literally "one who is separated" -- set apart from ordinary life for sacred purpose. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this with forms of *euchesthai* (to vow) and *hagnizein* (to purify), capturing both the volitional and purificatory dimensions. Crucially, this vow is available to both men and women (*ish o ishah*), making it one of the few forms of heightened sacred dedication open to women in the Torah's cultic system. The Nazirite vow is voluntary and temporary -- a chosen intensification of holiness rather than an assigned status.

**v. 3-4 -- The grape prohibition**: The prohibition extends far beyond wine to encompass the entire grapevine and everything it produces -- fresh grapes, raisins, grape juice, wine vinegar, and even the seeds and skins (*me-chartannim ve-ad zag*). The Leningrad Codex text is meticulous in its enumeration. This totality suggests the prohibition is not primarily about avoiding intoxication (which wine vinegar and grape skins would not cause) but about a symbolic boundary. The grapevine represents cultivation, agriculture, settled life, and its pleasures. The Nazirite temporarily renounces the full spectrum of civilized enjoyment that the vine represents. The LXX adds *oxos ex oinou* and *oxos ek sikera*, distinguishing wine-vinegar from other fermented-drink vinegar.

**v. 5 -- "they must let the hair of their head grow long and free"** (Heb. *gadel pera se'ar rosho*): The uncut hair is the visible, public sign of the Nazirite's consecration. The word *pera* means "wild" or "unrestrained" -- hair in its natural, untamed state. This stands in deliberate contrast to the grooming norms of ancient Near Eastern society, where trimmed and styled hair signified social order. The Nazirite's wild hair marks them as belonging to a different order -- set apart from normal social categories. There is a parallel with the high priest, who also bears a consecration on his head (the turban and golden plate, Exod 28:36-38), though the high priest's sign is crafted and the Nazirite's is organic. The term *qadosh* ("holy") applied to the Nazirite in this verse is the same term applied to YHWH himself.

**v. 6-7 -- Corpse avoidance, even for family**: The restriction against corpse contamination is more stringent for the Nazirite than for ordinary priests. Regular priests could defile themselves for immediate family members (Lev 21:1-3); only the high priest was prohibited from defiling himself even for his parents (Lev 21:11). The Nazirite thus temporarily assumes a purity status equivalent to the high priest -- an astonishing elevation for a lay person, and one available to women as well as men. The phrase *ki nezer elohav al rosho* ("because the consecration of his God is upon his head") directly parallels the language used of the high priest's crown (*nezer*) in Leviticus 21:12.

**v. 9 -- "If someone suddenly dies in their presence"** (Heb. *ve-ki yamut met alav be-feta pitom*): The Hebrew piles up terms for suddenness (*be-feta pitom* -- "in an instant, suddenly") to emphasize that this scenario involves no moral culpability. The contamination is accidental and unavoidable. Yet even accidental contact with death defiles the Nazirite's consecrated head and requires a complete restart of the vow period. This reveals a concept of holiness that is objective rather than merely moral -- holiness can be disrupted by contact with impurity regardless of intention. The purification process (seven days, then offerings on the eighth) mirrors the standard corpse-contamination purification of Numbers 19.

**v. 10-12 -- The restart procedure**: The offerings required -- two turtledoves or pigeons (a modest sacrifice), plus a lamb as a guilt offering (*asham*) -- address the violation from multiple angles. The birds serve as purification and burnt offering (restoring ritual status); the guilt offering addresses the objective debt incurred by the disrupted vow. Most significantly, all previous days of the Nazirite period are voided (*ve-ha-yamim ha-rishonim yippelu* -- literally "the former days shall fall," i.e., collapse, be annulled). The Nazirite must begin the entire vow from the start. This is not punishment but reflects the indivisible nature of the consecration: it cannot be partly fulfilled. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders the annulment with *alogoi* ("not counted"), capturing the concept clearly.

**v. 13-20 -- The completion ceremony**: The offerings at the conclusion of the vow are the most elaborate personal sacrificial sequence in the Torah: a burnt offering (male lamb), a purification offering (female lamb), a peace offering (ram), unleavened bread in two forms, plus grain and drink offerings. This surpasses even the offerings for a healed leper (Lev 14). The variety ensures that every category of sacrifice is represented -- the Nazirite's return to ordinary life is marked by a comprehensive engagement with the entire sacrificial system. The peace offering (*shelamim*), which is shared between God, priest, and offerer, is particularly significant as the culminating sacrifice: the Nazirite's reintegration into normal communal life is celebrated as a feast of wholeness (*shalom*).

**v. 18 -- "place it on the fire that burns under the peace offering"** (Heb. *ve-natan al ha-esh asher tachat zevach ha-shelamim*): The burning of the Nazirite's hair is one of the most symbolically rich moments in the Torah's ritual system. The hair -- which has been the visible sign of the vow throughout its duration, and which is described as *qodesh* (holy) -- is not simply discarded but offered to God on the altar fire. The consecrated hair ascends with the peace offering's smoke, returning to God what was dedicated to him. This is a literal offering of oneself -- the part of the body that grew during the period of holiness goes up to God. The Vaticanus text specifies placement *epi to pur* ("upon the fire"), consistent with the Hebrew.

**v. 20 -- "the Nazirite may drink wine"** (Heb. *ve-achar yishteh ha-nazir yayin*): This brief, almost anticlimactic statement marks the formal end of the Nazirite period. The very first prohibition -- wine -- is the last to be lifted, forming an inclusio (literary bracket) around the entire institution. The permission to drink wine signals the Nazirite's full return to ordinary life, with all its pleasures and responsibilities. The transition from sacred separation back to normal existence is not treated as a fall or diminishment but as a natural, ritually completed movement.

**v. 22-27 -- The Priestly Blessing** (*Birkat Kohanim*): These six verses contain perhaps the most consequential liturgical text in the Hebrew Bible. The blessing itself (vv. 24-26) consists of three lines of increasing length -- three words, five words, seven words in the Hebrew (3-5-7 pattern) -- creating a sense of expanding grace. Each line begins with the divine name YHWH, making the name the source and ground of every benediction. The tripartite structure became the foundation for later Trinitarian readings in Christian tradition, though the original context is firmly monotheistic -- the three lines represent three dimensions of divine favor, not three persons.

**v. 24 -- "May YHWH bless you and protect you"** (Heb. *yevarekha YHWH ve-yishmerekha*): The first line pairs blessing (*brk*) with guarding/protecting (*shmr*). The blessing invokes abundance, fertility, and flourishing; the protection invokes safety from harm, evil, and loss. Together they cover the full range of human need: positive provision and negative protection. The verbs are jussive (expressing wish or command directed to God), and the "you" is singular -- the blessing addresses each individual within the collective Israel, making it both communal and deeply personal.

**v. 25 -- "May YHWH make his face shine upon you and show you grace"** (Heb. *ya'er YHWH panav eleikha vi-chunekka*): The shining face is the central metaphor of divine favor throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. Pss 4:7, 31:17, 67:2, 80:4,8,20). A shining face means an open, welcoming, attentive countenance -- the opposite of a hidden or averted face, which signifies rejection or absence. The verb *channun* ("show grace") derives from *chen* -- unmerited favor, kindness that flows from the character of the giver rather than the merit of the receiver. This line moves from the visible (shining face) to the experienced (grace), from divine disposition to human reception.

**v. 26 -- "May YHWH lift up his face toward you and give you peace"** (Heb. *yissa YHWH panav eleikha ve-yasem lekha shalom*): The lifting of the face intensifies the image of verse 25. Where the face shines (general favor), the lifted face turns directly toward the individual -- full, personal attention from the Creator of the universe. The culminating word *shalom* is far richer than the English "peace" can convey. It encompasses wholeness, completeness, well-being, harmony, health, prosperity, and right relationship -- with God, with others, with oneself, with creation. That *shalom* is the final word of the blessing is theologically significant: the entire purpose of divine blessing, protection, favor, and attention is *shalom* -- the complete flourishing of the creature before its Creator.

**v. 27 -- "they will place my name upon the Sons of Israel"** (Heb. *ve-samu et shemi al benei yisrael*): This concluding verse reveals the mechanism of the blessing: the priests do not bless in their own authority but *place the divine name* upon the people. The name of YHWH -- which in Hebrew thought carries the reality and presence of the one named -- is transferred onto the community through the act of pronouncing the blessing. The priests are conduits, not sources. The final clause -- *va-ani avarakhem* ("and I myself will bless them") -- is emphatic. The pronoun *ani* ("I") is stated explicitly (unnecessary in Hebrew grammar, where the verb alone carries the subject), stressing that the ultimate agent of blessing is YHWH himself, not the priestly intermediaries. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this with *kai ego eulogeseo autous*, preserving the emphatic first-person pronoun.

**Structural note**: Numbers 6 pairs two seemingly unrelated institutions -- the Nazirite vow (vv. 1-21) and the Priestly Blessing (vv. 22-27) -- in a deliberate sequence. The Nazirite vow describes how an individual Israelite can voluntarily intensify their holiness, temporarily assuming a consecration that parallels the high priest's. The Priestly Blessing describes how holiness flows outward from YHWH through the priests to the entire community. Together they articulate a vision of holiness that moves in both directions: inward (the individual draws closer to God through voluntary separation) and outward (God's blessing radiates through appointed channels to all the people). The chapter thus completes the purity legislation of Numbers 5 by showing holiness not only as a boundary to be defended against contamination but as a living force that can be sought, intensified, and received. The Vaticanus LXX text tracks the Masoretic closely throughout, with the most notable difference being slightly expanded phrasing in the sacrificial lists of vv. 14-15, typical of the LXX's tendency toward clarifying enumeration in ritual contexts.
